I lived with Master Hugh's family for seven years. During that time, I taught myself to read and write. No one helped me. I had to find my own way.

When I first arrived, my mistress was kind. She began to teach me the alphabet. But her husband stopped her. He said that if a slave learned to read, he would want to be free. He said education and slavery could not exist together. He was right.

After that, my mistress changed. She became afraid of me with a book. If she saw me with a newspaper, she would rush at me and snatch it away. I was watched carefully at all times.

But it was too late. She had already given me the alphabet. That small gift was enough. I was determined to learn more.

I made friends with the poor white boys on the street. I traded bread for lessons. I always carried bread from the house, because I had more food than many of those boys. They taught me words. I taught them nothing — but they gave me the greatest gift I ever received.

I was about twelve years old. The thought of being a slave for life was heavy in my heart. Then I found a book called "The Columbian Orator." I read it again and again. It had a story of a slave who argued for his freedom — and won. Those words lit a fire inside me.

The more I read, the more I understood my situation. Reading gave me words to describe my pain. But it also made the pain worse. I could see clearly that I was a prisoner. I could see freedom — but I could not reach it.

I heard the word "abolitionist" many times. I did not know what it meant at first. Slowly, I learned. Abolitionists were people who wanted to end slavery. That word became the most important word in my world.

One day at the harbor, I helped two Irish workers unload heavy stone. When we finished, one man asked me, "Are you a slave for life?" I said yes. He looked sad. He told me to go north. He said I would find friends there. I was afraid he might be trying to trick me. But I remembered his words.

From that day, I had one plan: I would run away. But first, I needed to learn to write. I might need to write my own travel pass.

I learned to write from watching the men at the shipyard. They marked pieces of wood with letters: L for left, S for right. I copied those letters on fences and walls with chalk. Then I challenged boys to writing contests. "I can write as well as you," I would say. When they doubted me, I showed them what I knew. They corrected me and taught me more.

Every Monday, my mistress went to church and left me alone in the house. I used those hours to practice writing in my young master's old copybooks. I copied his handwriting over and over.

After years of secret effort, I could read and write. No one gave me permission. No one helped me. I took that freedom for myself — one letter at a time.
